name: pluggy (Plugin System for Python)

performance.notes: Negligible overhead; Python function call dispatch

agent_readiness.notes: Plugin system extracted from pytest. import pluggy. hookspec = pluggy.HookspecMarker('myproject'). hookimpl = pluggy.HookimplMarker('myproject'). class MySpec: @hookspec. def my_hook(self, arg): pass. class MyPlugin: @hookimpl. def my_hook(self, arg): return arg * 2. pm = pluggy.PluginManager('myproject'). pm.add_hookspecs(MySpec). pm.register(MyPlugin()). results = pm.hook.my_hook(arg=5). results → [10]. pm.get_plugins(). pm.is_registered(plugin). Hook wrappers: @hookimpl(wrapper=True). Historic hooks. firstresult=True for single result. tryfirst/trylast for ordering.

gotchas: Project name must match between HookspecMarker and HookimplMarker — typos cause silent failures
Hook return values are collected in reverse registration order — list ordering can surprise
firstresult=True stops at first non-None return — rest not called
Wrapper hooks must use yield — not a regular return
Limited documentation examples beyond basic use cases
best_when: Building extensible Python applications that support plugins
Framework development where third-party plugins should hook into lifecycle events
When pytest-style plugin architecture is a known pattern for your team
Replacing manual callback registries with a principled hook system
avoid_when: Simple single-app use cases — plain functions or callbacks are simpler
When a full plugin discovery mechanism (entry_points) is needed alongside pluggy
summary: pluggy is pytest's plugin hook system, extracted for general use. HookspecMarker + HookimplMarker define the contract. PluginManager.register(plugin) + pm.hook.my_hook(). Results collected from all implementations. MIT by pytest-dev. Type-annotated. Best for extensible Python frameworks. Project name must match in markers. Hook ordering via tryfirst/trylast. firstresult=True for single-result hooks. Used internally by pytest, tox, devpi.
